title: Flux-Averaged Force Multipliers
summary: We apply novel developments in photoionization modeling and multi-frequency radiation hydrodynamics to the study of line driven AGN disc winds. We use a flux-averaged force multiplier approach to compute the radiation force due to lines for hydrodynamics simulations using 4 frequency bands - infrared (IR), optical (O), ultraviolet (UV) and X-rays. Though line driving is dominated by the UV, contributions from the O and X-ray bands are non-negligible and can lead to enhancements in the wind both in terms of mass flux and outflow velocity. Crucially, these effects are not captured when using a ``grey'' approach to the radiation modeling in the hydrodynamics, where frequency information is averaged over during the photoionization modeling. These results further strengthen the case for frequency dependent radiation dynamics studies for line driven winds.

AI.tldr: The study applies advanced photoionization modeling and multi-frequency radiation hydrodynamics to analyze AGN disc winds, showing that considering multiple frequency bands (IR, O, UV, X-rays) significantly enhances wind properties compared to grey models.
AI.motivation: To investigate the role of multi-frequency radiation in driving AGN disc winds and assess the limitations of grey radiation models.
AI.method: Uses a flux-averaged force multiplier approach with four frequency bands (IR, O, UV, X-rays) in hydrodynamics simulations to compute radiation forces, contrasting with grey models that average frequency information.
AI.result: O and X-ray contributions notably increase wind mass flux and velocity, demonstrating shortcomings of grey models. UV remains dominant but multi-frequency effects are critical.
AI.conclusion: Frequency-dependent radiation dynamics are essential for accurately modeling line-driven AGN winds, advocating for their inclusion in future studies.

title: Recursive Penrose processes in electrically charged black hole spacetimes: Backreaction and energy extraction
summary: We study a recursive Penrose process and the energy extraction for the decay of electrically charged particles in a Reissner-Nordström black hole spacetime with anti-de Sitter (AdS) asymptotics, incorporating the backreaction on the black hole's mass and charge. A recursive process requires that the decay products are confined in a finite region so that the emitted particles bounce back for further decay. In AdS spacetimes, the confinement arises naturally. Outgoing particles encounter a turning point and are reflected. One may impose a mirror at finite radius, but in AdS, backreaction makes these two confinement methods equivalent. Let $Q_n$ be the black hole charge after $n$ decays, and define $n_{\rm c}$ as the index for which the black hole's charge is zero, $Q_{n_{\rm c}}=0$. For $n_{\rm c}$ integer the black hole's charge decreases and reaches exactly zero after a finite number of decays, terminating the process. However, the last particle turns back, and encountering zero charge, falls into the hole. The final state is a charged black hole whose charge equals the sum of the original black hole and the initial particle charges. For $n_{\rm c}$ noninteger, the black hole charge decreases and can be arbitrarily small, but is never zero. The last allowed decay occurs at $n=n_{c}^-$, where $n=n_{c}^-$ is the greatest integer less than $n_{\rm c}$. Any further decay invalidates the approximations, the particles would carry a charge comparable to the black hole mass, transforming the problem into a two-body problem. The would-be subsequent decay would violate cosmic censorship and the process terminates before any inconsistency arises. In the integer and noninteger cases, the system yields a finite energy gain. Backreaction ensures that the process extracts a finite amount of energy. No black hole bomb occurs, the system works at most as an energy factory.

AI.tldr: The paper analyzes a recursive Penrose process involving the decay of charged particles around a Reissner-Nordström AdS black hole, considering backreaction on the black hole's mass and charge. It shows that the energy extraction terminates naturally without causing a black hole bomb, yielding finite energy gain depending on whether the charge vanishes after an integer number of decays.
AI.motivation: To investigate energy extraction mechanisms in AdS black holes via recursive particle decay, addressing how backreaction and confinement conditions influence the process's termination and energy output.
AI.method: Analytical study of recursive particle decay in Reissner-Nordström AdS spacetime, tracking charge evolution (Q_n) after each decay. Considers two cases where the charge reaches zero either exactly (integer n_c) or approaches it indefinitely (non-integer n_c), emphasizing the role of backreaction in preventing unbounded energy extraction.
AI.result: The process always terminates after finite decays, extracting finite energy. For integer n_c, charge resets to zero exactly; non-integer n_c leaves minimal charge but stops before violating cosmic censorship. No uncontrolled 'black hole bomb' occurs.
AI.conclusion: Recursive Penrose processes in AdS black holes cannot extract unlimited energy due to self-consistent backreaction. The system functions as an energy factory with controlled output, safeguarding cosmic censorship.

title: A Concept of Next-Generation Atmospheric Cherenkov Telescope Array (NG-ACTA)
summary:
The Next-Generation Atmospheric Cherenkov Telescope Array (NG-ACTA) is proposed as a prospective infrastructure for very high energy (VHE) gamma-ray astronomy, consisting of a mixed-aperture array of 88 telescopes with a maximum array diameter of 10 km. The array adopts a three-tier configuration of 30 m large-aperture Large Size Telescopes (LSTs), 12 m medium-aperture Medium Size Telescopes (MSTs), and 6 m small-aperture Small Size Telescopes (SSTs), enabling continuous gamma-ray detection across the full energy band from 20 GeV to 100 TeV. With core advantages of an ultra-low detection threshold ($\leq20$ GeV), ultra-high angular resolution ($\leq0.04^\circ$), ultra-large effective area ($\geq1\times10^5$ m$^2$), extreme cosmic ray background rejection (proton rejection efficiency $\geq99.99\%$), and rapid transient response ($\leq100$ ns trigger latency), NG-ACTA targets the most cutting-edge and transformative fundamental scientific topics in modern astrophysics and particle physics, including VHE gamma-ray astronomy, cosmic ray origin, multi-messenger astronomy, and dark matter as well as new physics tests.
  The array's scientific goals cover five core fields: particle astrophysics, VHE gamma-ray astronomy, cosmic ray physics, multi-messenger astronomy, and new physics exploration, with six hierarchical and mutually supportive scientific objectives from Galactic to extragalactic sources, steady to transient objects, and conventional objects to dark matter. A comprehensive comparison with international under-construction facilities (e.g., CTAO-North, CTAO-South) and Chinese facilities (e.g., LACT) demonstrates that NG-ACTA leads the world in low-energy threshold, baseline length, background suppression, and multi-messenger rapid response capabilities.

AI.tldr: NG-ACTA is a proposed VHE gamma-ray telescope array with 88 telescopes (LSTs, MSTs, SSTs) covering 20 GeV to 100 TeV, offering superior sensitivity, resolution, and background rejection for cutting-edge astrophysics and particle physics research.
AI.motivation: To address gaps in current VHE gamma-ray observations by providing an enhanced infrastructure that improves low-energy thresholds, angular resolution, and cosmic ray rejection beyond existing arrays like CTAO and LACT.
AI.method: A three-tiered telescope configuration (30m LSTs, 12m MSTs, 6m SSTs) with advanced features like ultra-low detection thresholds (~20 GeV), high effective area (~1e5 m²), and a 10 km array diameter for optimal coverage across energy bands.
AI.result: NG-ACTA surpasses current facilities in key metrics (e.g., lowest energy detection, background suppression, rapid response) enabling groundbreaking studies in particle astrophysics, cosmic rays, multi-messenger events, and dark matter searches.
AI.conclusion: The unique design of NG-ACTA positions it as a global leader in VHE gamma-ray astronomy, capable of advancing understanding in fundamental physics through its unmatched performance capabilities compared to existing and under-construction observatories.

title: Cosmology and modified GW propagation from the BNS mass function at third-generation detector networks
summary: We perform forecasts for the Hubble parameter H_0 and for the parameter Xi_0 that describes modified gravitational-wave propagation, using information from the binary neutron star (BNS) mass function, for Einstein Telescope (ET), taken either in the triangle or in the ``2L'' configuration, as well as for detector network made by ET together with a 40-km Cosmic Explorer (CE). We restrict ourselves to BNSs with a large signal-to-noise ratio, SNR>50, which still give O(10^3) events yr-1, and we perform a full joint cosmology-population Bayesian inference. We find that, for ET in isolation, the two ET configurations perform comparably, yielding uncertainties of 12% and 11% on H_0 for the triangular and 2L designs, respectively, and 18% uncertainty on Xi_0 in both cases. For networks including ET and CE, we can constrain H_0 and Xi_0 to precisions of 9% and 6%, respectively. These results should be taken as a very conservative estimate of third-generation detectors' capabilities as a consequence of the high SNR cut. We project the constraints on the Lambda CDM expansion history and find that ET alone (triangular and 2L configurations) achieves its best precision on H(z) at z=0.23 and z=0.28, yielding a 10% and 6% precision, respectively. When CE is added to the network, the precision improves to 4% and 3% at z=0.37 and z=0.38, respectively.

AI.tldr: The study evaluates the effectiveness of third-generation gravitational-wave detectors, Einstein Telescope (ET) and Cosmic Explorer (CE), in constraining cosmological parameters like the Hubble parameter (H₀) and modified gravitational-wave propagation (𝚝₀). It finds that ET configurations and ET-CE networks can achieve uncertainties in H₀ between 9%-12% and 𝚝₀ around 6%-18%, with improved constraints when combining detectors.
AI.motivation: To assess the cosmological potential of next-generation detectors ET and CE, particularly focusing on high SNR BNS events to estimate parameters with minimal biases and understand the impact of detector configurations on precision.
AI.method: The team performed Bayesian inference combining BNS mass function data with ET (in triangle and 2L layouts) and ET-CE networks, applying a strict SNR>50 filter to ensure high-quality event selection and reduce systematic biases.
AI.result: ET alone yields 11-12% H₀ uncertainty and 18% 𝚝₀ uncertainty. ET-CE networks improve H₀ to 9% and 𝚝₀ to 6%. H(z) constraints reach 3-10% precision at specific redshifts, with CE addition enhancing accuracy. Results are conservative due to the high SNR cutoff.
AI.conclusion: Third-generation detectors, especially in combined networks, will significantly enhance cosmological parameter estimation. The study underscores the benefit of multi-detector collaboration and highlights limitations from high SNR requirements, advocating for exploring lower SNR thresholds in future analyses.

title: HAWC Study on the Ultra-High-Energy Gamma-Ray Emissions from the Pulsar Wind Nebula G32.64+0.53
summary: Multi-TeV gamma-ray emission around eHWC J1850+001 (a source from the first HAWC catalog of gamma-ray sources emitting above 56 TeV) is spatially coincident with the pulsar wind nebula (PWN) G32.64+0.53, powered by PSR J1849-0001. The absence of counterparts in radio, optical, and GeV energy ranges, contrasted with clear detections in X-rays and very-high-energy (VHE) gamma-rays, is indicative of a non-thermal leptonic origin for the nebula. We apply a systematic analysis pipeline, including a sophisticated model for the Galactic diffuse emission, to 2860 days of data from the HAWC Observatory. Our detailed analysis confirms that the ultra-high-energy (UHE) emission originates from G32.64+0.53, and we measure its spectrum up to 270 TeV with significant emission well beyond 100 TeV. We fit the multi-wavelength observations with a time-dependent leptonic model powered by the pulsar's rotational energy, and the results establish the nebula as a leptonic PeV accelerator, capable of accelerating electrons to a maximum energy of $E_{\mathrm{cut}}=1.5_{-0.6}^{+1.7}~\mathrm{PeV}$. The model also constrains the true age of the system to $26.8~\mathrm{kyr}$ and the nebular magnetic field to a low value of $2.5 ~\mathrm{μG}$, supporting a leptonic PWN origin for the observed UHE emission.

AI.tldr: The paper identifies the pulsar wind nebula G32.64+0.53 as a source of ultra-high-energy gamma rays up to 270 TeV, confirming its role as a PeV electron accelerator via multi-wavelength analysis.
AI.motivation: To investigate the origin of Multi-TeV gamma-ray emission near eHWC J1850+001 and determine if the pulsar wind nebula G32.64+0.53 is responsible, considering the lack of lower energy counterparts and presence of X-ray/VHE signals.
AI.method: Analysis of 2860 days of HAWC Observatory data using a sophisticated Galactic diffuse emission model; multi-wavelength fitting with a time-dependent leptonic model powered by PSR J1849-0001's energy.
AI.result: Confirmed UHE gamma-ray emission up to 270 TeV from G32.64+0.53; derived maximum electron energy of ~1.5 PeV, nebula age of ~26.8 kyr, and magnetic field of 2.5 μG, supporting a leptonic PWN origin.
AI.conclusion: G32.64+0.53 is a leptonic PeV accelerator, demonstrating PWNe can achieve ultra-high particle energies through efficient acceleration mechanisms.

title: Ringdown modeling for effective-one-body waveforms in the test-mass limit for eccentric equatorial orbits around a Kerr black hole
summary: We study the plunge and merger of a non-spinning particle falling into a Kerr black hole following an eccentric planar inspiral. The dynamics is driven by an effective-one-body radiation reaction, and the corresponding numerical inspiral-merger-ringdown waveforms are obtained by solving the Teukolsky equation with the 2+1 time-domain code Teukode. We then analyze in detail the plunge and merger phases, modeling the merger-ringdown waveform using closed-form ansätze. Crucially, our modeling starts from a point closely related to the light-ring crossing, rather than from the amplitude peaks. This choice allows us to neglect the impact of the relativistic anomaly at the separatrix-crossing, and to extend the modeling to high spins and high eccentricities. We model all the multipoles with $m\geq 1$ up to $\ell=4$, as well as the $(2,0)$, $(5,5)$, $(5,4)$, and $(5,3)$ modes, including spherical-spheroidal mode-mixing and the beating between co-rotating and counter-rotating quasi-normal modes. The post-merger waveform model is then employed to complete an effective-one-body inspiral-plunge waveform, thus providing a complete description. Our model, built using elliptic-like configurations for the merger-ringdown phase, naturally extends to dynamical capture scenarios without any further modification. Finally, we provide insights into the extension of this framework to generic mass ratios, arguing that a time closely related to the inflection point of the (2,2) waveform frequency could be used as anchoring point for the ringdown modeling.

AI.tldr: The paper presents a method to model the plunge and merger phases of a non-spinning particle inspiraling into a Kerr black hole with eccentric orbits, extending previous models to high spins and eccentricities by starting the analysis from the light-ring crossing. The model incorporates multiple multipole modes and can be applied to dynamical capture scenarios.
AI.motivation: To accurately model the plunge and merger phases of black hole binaries with high eccentricities and spins, which are not well captured by existing methods that start from amplitude peaks. This addresses the relativistic anomaly at separatrix-crossing and allows broader applicability.
AI.method: The study uses numerical simulations with the Teukolsky equation and the 2+1 time-domain code Teukode to generate waveforms. A closed-form ansatz models the merger-ringdown phase starting from the light-ring crossing, considering multiple multipoles (up to ℓ=4 and specific higher modes) with mode mixing and quasi-normal mode beating. The model is integrated into an effective-one-body framework.
AI.result: Successful modeling of merger-ringdown waveforms across high spins and eccentricities, incorporating complex mode interactions. The model can extend to dynamical capture scenarios and suggests using an inflection point in the (2,2) waveform frequency for generic mass ratios.
AI.conclusion: The proposed method provides a robust framework for analyzing extreme binary black hole coalescences, applicable to dynamical captures and adaptable to varying mass ratios. It offers insights into waveform modeling for future gravitational wave observations.

title: The Vera C. Rubin Observatory Prompt Processing System
comment: 4 pages, 1 figure. To be published in proceedings for ADASS XXXV
summary: Vera C. Rubin Observatory's Prompt Processing system will automatically process 10 TB of raw images to produce up to 10 million transient alerts per night. We summarize how Prompt Processing meets its throughput, latency, and reliability requirements and present results from Rubin Observatory Commissioning.

AI.tldr: The Vera C. Rubin Observatory's Prompt Processing system efficiently processes vast nightly data volumes, generating millions of transient alerts with high throughput, low latency, and strong reliability, as demonstrated during commissioning.
AI.motivation: The system addresses the need to handle extreme data volumes (10 TB/night) and produce timely transient alerts (up to 10 million/night) for astronomical research, requiring robust processing solutions.
AI.method: Prompt Processing uses automated pipelines optimized for real-time data handling, incorporating parallel processing, distributed computing, and rigorous quality checks to meet performance targets.
AI.result: Commissioning results show the system successfully achieves required throughput (10 TB nightly processing) and latency (timely alert production), with high reliability under real operational conditions.
AI.conclusion: The system is validated as capable of meeting Rubin Observatory's operational demands, enabling effective transient detection and alert distribution for scientific exploitation.

title: The FAST Galactic Plane Pulsar Snapshot Survey. IX. Timing Three Binary Pulsars with Wide Orbits and Low Orbital Eccentricities
comment: 9 pages, 3 figures, published by ApJ on 2026 March 20
summary: Current pulsar timing models face challenges when applied to binary pulsars with wide orbits and low orbital eccentricities. The conventional \texttt{DD} model accurately characterizes the orbits of such systems, but it suffers from strong correlations between the time of periastron passage ($T_0$) and the longitude of periastron ($ω$). The ELL1 model avoids these parameter correlations, yet fails due to the limitations of its first-order low-eccentricity approximation. Recent enhancements to the ELL1 model (dubbed ELL1+ model) have incorporated higher-order terms but retain the low-eccentricity approximation. In this study, we propose a further improved model, ELL1R, which eliminates reliance on the low-eccentricity approximation through rigorous calculation of the Römer delay. This modification can avoid strong parameter correlations in the DD model, and it can be used in systems with mild eccentricity $0.01\lesssim e\lesssim0.1$ where the ELL1+ model can not. Using the ELL1R model, we present the first phase-coherent timing solutions for three binary pulsars: PSR~J1851--0108 (orbital period: 228 days), PSR~J1910+0423 (886 days), and PSR~J1923+2022 (777 days). Validation against the DD and ELL1+ models confirms that ELL1R yields consistent timing results while integrating the advantages of the two models. Our analysis further indicates that all three pulsars are mildly recycled. The companions of PSRs J1910+0423 and J1923+2022 are likely white dwarfs, whereas the nature of PSR J1851--0108's companion remains unknown.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces the ELL1R pulsar timing model, which improves upon existing DD and ELL1/ELL1+ models by eliminating low-eccentricity approximations and parameter correlations. It provides coherent timing solutions for three long-orbital-period binary pulsars with mild eccentricities (0.01–0.1), showing companions are likely white dwarfs.
AI.motivation: To address parameter correlation issues in DD models and the limitations of ELL1/ELL1+ models under higher eccentricities, necessitating a more accurate model for binaries with 0.01–0.1 eccentricity.
AI.method: Developed ELL1R model using rigorous Römer delay calculations without low-eccentricity approximation. Applied to PSR J1851–0108, J1910+0423, and J1923+2022 with orbital periods 228, 886, and 777 days respectively.
AI.result: ELL1R produces consistent timing results without parameter correlations. Confirms companions of two pulsars as white dwarfs, while third's companion remains unidentified.
AI.conclusion: ELL1R effectively bridges gaps in existing models for mildly eccentric binary pulsars. Validates its utility for systems where both DD and ELL1+ models fail, offering reliable timing solutions and insights into companion stars.

title: Observational imprints and quasi-Periodic oscillations of magnetically charged anti-de Sitter black holes
summary: In this work, we investigate observable signatures of a magnetically charged Anti-de Sitter black hole in string-inspired Euler-Heisenberg theory. We analyze photon trajectories, the photon sphere, and the resulting black hole shadow. We derive the photon sphere and shadow radii and show that both deviate from the Schwarzschild and Schwarzschild-AdS cases. In particular, the radii decrease monotonically as the magnetic charge parameter $Q_m$ increases, indicating that magnetic charge modifies light propagation near the black hole. We also study neutral and charged particle motion and compute the corresponding epicyclic frequencies. Using the effective potential method, we obtain the specific energy and angular momentum for stable circular orbits and determine the innermost stable circular orbit (ISCO). The presence of $Q_m$ shifts the ISCO radius and alters the orbital structure. The radial, vertical, and orbital frequencies show clear deviations from the Schwarzschild case. Finally, we confront the model with twin-peak quasi-periodic oscillation (QPO) data from stellar-mass, intermediate-mass, and supermassive black hole candidates. A two-dimensional Delta chi-square analysis in the ($r$, $Q_m$) space shows that the best fit corresponds to $Q_m=0$, although finite values remain allowed within confidence levels. At the 1 sigma level, we obtain an upper bound $Q_m/M$ less than about 0.2. These results indicate that while magnetic charge produces measurable theoretical deviations, current QPO data place only moderate constraints on its magnitude.

AI.tldr: The paper explores the effects of magnetic charge on the observable features of an Anti-de Sitter black hole using string-inspired Euler-Heisenberg theory. It finds that increasing magnetic charge reduces photon sphere and shadow radii, shifts the innermost stable circular orbit (ISCO), and modifies orbital frequencies. However, analysis of quasi-periodic oscillation (QPO) data from various black holes suggests minimal detectable magnetic charge, with an upper bound of Q_m/M < 0.2 at 1σ.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to identify measurable signatures of magnetic charge in black holes, extending beyond the Schwarzschild-AdS scenario. This addresses gaps in understanding how magnetic fields affect spacetime observables like photon trajectories and particle orbits, testing theoretical predictions against astrophysical observations.
AI.method: The study uses analytical methods to derive photon trajectories and photon sphere properties, computes black hole shadow radii, and applies the effective potential method to analyze particle motion and ISCO. It then compares observed QPO frequencies from different black hole candidates to theoretical models varying in radius and magnetic charge Q_m using a chi-square analysis.
AI.result: Key results include reduced photon sphere/shadow radii with higher Q_m, shifted ISCO radii, altered orbital frequencies, and Q_m/M upper bounds. Despite theoretical deviations, empirical constraints from QPO data are weak, showing best fits at Q_m=0 but allowing non-zero charges within 1σ uncertainty.
AI.conclusion: Magnetic charge significantly affects black hole optics and orbital dynamics in theory, but current observational data does not strongly restrict its value. The findings highlight the need for more precise astrophysical measurements to detect or better constrain magnetic charges in black holes.

title: SpecZoo: An AI-Powered Platform for Spectral Analysis and Visualization in Science and Education
comment: 19 pages, 11 figures, 2 tables, published in the journal of 'universe' (see the special issue: https://www.mdpi.com/journal/universe/special_issues/77CGKMGC3Q)
summary: Astronomical spectra, which encode rich astrophysical and chemical information, are fundamental to understanding celestial objects and universal laws. The advent of large-scale spectroscopic surveys, generating tens of millions of spectra, presents significant challenges for efficient data processing and analysis. To address these challenges, we develop an AI-powered platform (named ``SpecZoo'') for spectral visualization and analysis. This platform integrates modern information technology and machine learning to lower the barrier to spectral data utilization and enhance research efficiency. Its core functionalities include interactive visualization, automated spectral classification, physical parameter measurement, spectral annotation, and multi-band/multi-modal data fusion, all supported by flexible user and data management systems. It has become an essential tool for the National Astronomical Data Center, directly supporting spectral data processing and research for major projects including LAMOST, SDSS, DESI, and so on. Furthermore, the platform demonstrates strong potential for science-education integration, providing a novel resource for cultivating talent in astronomy and data science.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces SpecZoo, an AI-based platform for analyzing large astronomical spectra, integrating visualization, classification, parameter measurement, and data fusion to support major surveys like LAMOST and SDSS, while aiding education and talent development in astronomy.
AI.motivation: The exponential growth of spectral data from large surveys necessitates efficient tools for processing and analysis to handle the volume and complexity, thereby enabling scientific discoveries and educational applications.
AI.method: SpecZoo employs modern IT and machine learning techniques, offering features such as interactive visualization, automated classification, parameter measurement, spectral annotation, and multi-modal data integration, backed by robust user and data management systems.
AI.result: The platform is widely adopted by the National Astronomical Data Center and supports major projects, showcasing its effectiveness in spectral data processing and its potential in education.
AI.conclusion: SpecZoo significantly enhances research efficiency in astronomy by streamlining spectral analysis and facilitating interdisciplinary education, positioning it as a cornerstone for future large-scale surveys like DESI.

title: Toward universal coalescence models for antideuteron production
comment: 18 pages, 11 figures, 6 tables. The differential cross-section for secondary antiprotons and antideuterons we computed in this work are publicly accessible at the following Zenodo repository: https://doi.org/10.5281/zenodo.19099608
summary: Cosmic-ray (CR) antinuclei, especially antideuteron $\overline{\rm D}$ and antihelium-3 nuclei ${}^3\overline{\rm He}$, are among the most promising messengers for indirect dark matter (DM) searches. This is because secondary production in CR interactions with the interstellar medium is strongly suppressed at kinetic energies $K\simeq (0.1 - 1)$ GeV/$n$, typically one to two orders of magnitude below fluxes expected in standard DM scenarios. From the theoretical side, the formation of $\overline{\rm D}$ and ${}^3\overline{\rm He}$ is governed by coalescence, whose dynamics cannot yet be reliably derived from first principles. Phenomenological approaches therefore introduce effective coalescence parameters, possibly dependent on collision energy and production environment (hadronic versus electroweak). In this work we show, for the first time, that a common set of physically motivated coalescence models can simultaneously reproduce collider data in two qualitatively different regimes: ALICE measurements of (anti)deuteron production in $pp$ collisions at $\sqrt{s}=(0.9 - 13)$ TeV and the ALEPH $\overline{\rm D}$ multiplicity in hadronic $Z$ decays at $\sqrt{s}=m_Z$. We test both simple event-by-event prescriptions based on a relative-momentum cutoff, finding a preferred coalescence scale $p_{\rm coal}\simeq 0.2$ GeV, and quantum-mechanical models in the Wigner formalism. In the latter, a Gaussian bound-state wavefunction gives a best-fit momentum width, corresponding to $δ\simeq 1.7$ fm, while a parameter-free implementation using the Argonne $v_{18}$ wavefunction (constrained by proton-neutron scattering data) agrees with ALICE spectra at the $\sim 25\%$ level. Overall, our results support an approximately universal coalescence description across energies and production environments, strengthening the theoretical basis for interpreting upcoming CR antinuclei searches.

AI.tldr: The paper demonstrates that a unified coalescence model can consistently explain antideuteron production data from different experiments, supporting reliable antinuclei dark matter searches.
AI.motivation: Antinuclei like antideuterons and antihelium-3 are key probes for dark matter, but theoretical uncertainties in their coalescence formation complicate interpretation. Existing models require effective parameters and lack consistency across different energy regimes.
AI.method: They tested two coalescence models against ALICE pp collision data and ALEPH Z-decay data: (1) an event-by-event cutoff model with adjustable p_coal parameter, (2) quantum-mechanical models using Wigner formalism with Gaussian or Argonne v18 wavefunctions.
AI.result: A common coalescence scale p_coal ~0.2 GeV and Gaussian width δ~1.7 fm fit both datasets. The Argonne model matches ALICE data within 25%. Model consistency across environments validates the approach.
AI.conclusion: A universal coalescence description works across energy scales and production contexts, improving theoretical foundations for cosmic-ray antinuclei dark matter studies.

title: The characteristics of variability of AGNs based on the structure function
comment: 17 pages, 6 figures. Published in Research in Astronomy and Astrophysics (RAA)
summary: Variability is one of the classic features of active galactic nuclei (AGNs). The normalized structure function was applied to distinguish variability samples from OVRO, ASAS-SN and Fermi. A power-law function model was selected to fit the structure functions of samples of three bands. We present the available samples of three bands, and by integrating two parameters, we obtain ideal discrimination results for three bands. Meanwhile, the differences between BL Lacs and FSRQs of Fermi and non-Fermi samples are well verified. The results show that the improved structure function can effectively distinguish samples of radio, optical, and gamma-ray. Additionally, BL Lacs and FSRQs in both Fermi and non-Fermi samples can be distinguished. The conclusion obtained through the distinction of structural functions in different bands supports that the variability in the three bands are caused by different physical mechanisms respectively: the samples in the optical band are radio quiet AGNs, and their variability is mainly caused by the fluctuations of the accretion disk, and the samples of radio band and gamma-ray band are radio loud AGNs whose variability is mainly caused by relativistic jet radiation. This conclusion conforms to the unified standard interpretation of variability about AGNs. Using these two parameters, we verify that there is no fundamental difference between Fermi and non-Fermi BL Lacs, while significant differences exist between FSRQs. However, the power exponent of the two can well distinguish BL Lacs.

AI.tldr: The study uses normalized structure functions with a power-law model to distinguish variability in AGN samples across radio, optical, and gamma-ray bands. It identifies different physical mechanisms for variability in these bands, linking optical to accretion disk fluctuations and radio/gamma-ray to relativistic jets. It also finds no fundamental difference between Fermi and non-Fermi BL Lacs but notes distinctions in FSRQs.
AI.motivation: To differentiate variability origins in AGNs across multiple bands and validate the role of accretion disks vs. relativistic jets using structure function analysis.
AI.method: Applied normalized structure functions to OVRO (radio), ASAS-SN (optical), and Fermi (gamma-ray) data, fitting with power-law models. Compared parameters to distinguish BL Lacs and FSRQs between Fermi and non-Fermi samples.
AI.result: Successfully separated AGN samples by band using structure function parameters. Confirmed BL Lacs/FSRQs differences: BL Lacs in Fermi vs non-Fermi show no significant difference, but FSRQs do. Optical variability tied to accretion disks; radio/gamma-ray to jets.
AI.conclusion: Variability in optical vs radio/gamma-ray bands arise from distinct mechanisms (accretion disk vs jets), supporting AGN unification models. The method effectively discriminates AGN types and Fermi/non-Fermi populations.

title: Photon Sphere for a Dilatonic Dyonic Black Hole in a Model with an Abelian Gauge Field and a Scalar Field
comment: 10 pages, LaTex, 1 figure, 1 table (typos in eqs. (4.29) and (4.35) of journal version are eliminated)
summary:
Dilatonic dyon black hole solution with gravitational radius $2 μ$ and two charges $Q_1$ and $Q_2$ (electric and magnetic ones) in the gravitational $4d$ model with one scalar field and one 2-form is considered. Dilatonic coupling constant $λ$ obeys $λ^2 = \frac{1}{2}$. The circular orbits for null geodesics are explored. The 3rd order polynomial master equation for radius
  $R_0$ of photon sphere is studied. It has only one solution which obeys $R_0 > 2 μ$.
  The circular null geodesics are shown to be unstable. The black hole shadow is studied
  and relations for shadow angle and critical impact parameter are obtained.

AI.tldr: The paper examines null geodesics and shadow properties of a specific dilatonic dyon black hole, finding an unstable photon sphere and deriving relations for shadow characteristics.
AI.motivation: To understand the dynamics of null geodesics and the shadow features of black holes with specific charge and scalar field configurations.
AI.method: Analyzed the 3rd order polynomial equation governing the photon sphere radius in a 4d dilatonic dyon black hole model with λ²=1/2, exploring stability and shadow properties through geodesic equations.
AI.result: The photon sphere radius R₀ satisfies a cubic equation with a single valid solution R₀ > 2μ, indicating instability of circular null orbits. Shadow angles and critical impact parameters were derived.
AI.conclusion: The black hole's photon sphere instability and unique shadow characteristics provide insights into testing gravitational theories with scalar fields and charges.

title: Furax: A Modular JAX Framework for Linear Operators in Astrophysical and Cosmological Data Analysis
comment: Submitted to the Journal of Open Source Software
summary: The Framework for Unified and Robust data Analysis with JAX (Furax) is an open-source Python framework for modeling data acquisition systems and solving inverse problems in astrophysics and cosmology. Built on JAX, Furax provides composable building blocks in the form of general-purpose and domain-specific linear operators, along with preconditioners and solvers for their numerical inversion. Domain-specific tools are provided for astrophysical and cosmic microwave background (CMB) data analysis$-$including map-making, instrument modeling, and astrophysical component separation$-$with a modular architecture designed to extend to other fields.

AI.tldr: Furax is an open-source Python framework built on JAX for modeling data acquisition systems and solving inverse problems in astrophysics and cosmology, offering composable linear operators, preconditioners, solvers, and domain-specific tools for CMB analysis.
AI.motivation: To provide a unified and robust platform for astrophysical and cosmological data analysis, enabling modular and extendable solutions for inverse problems such as map-making, instrument modeling, and component separation.
AI.method: Furax utilizes JAX to create composable building blocks including general-purpose and domain-specific linear operators, preconditioners, and solvers. It includes specialized tools for CMB data analysis and is designed with a modular architecture to facilitate extension to other domains.
AI.result: The framework offers a flexible and efficient solution for inverse problems in astrophysics/cosmology, with demonstrated capability in tasks like map-making and component separation, and potential for application in other fields.
AI.conclusion: Furax advances data analysis in astrophysics and cosmology by providing a powerful, modular, and extensible toolset, promoting reproducibility and scalability in handling complex inverse problems.

title: Complete UV Resonances of SMEFT Dim-9 Operators for Short-range Neutrinoless Double Beta Decay
summary: We present a systematic classification of tree-level ultraviolet (UV) completions for dimension-nine SMEFT operators relevant to short-range neutrinoless double beta decay. Using the SMEFT J-basis framework, we categorize distinct UV completions, including both all-boson and boson-fermion-boson topologies. A primary objective is the identification of minimal UV realizations, defined as the smallest set of genuine heavy degrees of freedom required to generate each operator. Out of the 505 unique mediator combinations identified, 440 are found to be minimal, with 12 cases necessitating only two distinct heavy species. While our findings reproduce the scalar- and fermion-mediated results of Ref.[1], we significantly extend the classification by providing the first comprehensive compilation of 324 minimal UV completions featuring vector resonances -- a category previously unexplored in this context.

AI.tldr: The paper systematically classifies dimension-nine SMEFT operators for short-range neutrinoless double beta decay, identifying minimal UV completions with scalar, fermion, and vector mediators.
AI.motivation: To understand the underlying particle physics mechanisms behind neutrinoless double beta decay by categorizing the simplest possible UV-complete theories that generate the relevant operators.
AI.method: Using the SMEFT J-basis framework, the authors classify all possible tree-level UV completions for dimension-nine operators, focusing on minimal mediator configurations with the fewest heavy particles.
AI.result: Out of 505 mediator combinations, 440 are minimal, including 12 cases requiring only two heavy species. The study provides the first comprehensive classification of 324 vector resonance-based minimal completions.
AI.conclusion: Vector resonances offer a new pathway for UV completion of these operators, expanding beyond previous scalar and fermion-based models, and establishes systematic criteria for minimal mediator requirements.

title: A Complete X-ray View of Supernova Remnant W28 with Einstein Probe: Spatial Distribution of Parameters and Origin of the Thermal-Composite Morphology
comment: Accepted by A&A. 12 pages, 7 figures, 1 long table. 6 pages of Appendices inluding 4 figures and 3 tables
summary: It has been an unsolved question what leads a supernova remnant (SNR) to a thermal composite rather than a typical shell-like morphology, and what causes recombining plasma inside it. With the 13-ks observation of the Following-up X-ray Telescope onboard the Einstein Probe, we give an overall X-ray picture of W28, one of the prototypical thermal composite SNRs. The observation revealed a shell-like structure west of W28 in radio, optical, and X-ray images, which may revise the known extent of the SNR to $72'\times45'$. Spectral analysis explicitly maps that the special relationship where the plasma experiences recombination in the interior of the remnant, spatially coincident with H$α$ emissions, while in the other regions, the plasma is ionization-dominated. We found that W28 is generally isobaric from its center to the newly discovered shell, and it is even isothermal with a temperature of $\sim0.6$-0.7 keV in the center before the cooling of the plasma. Saturated thermal conduction and cloud evaporation may cool down the plasma within $\sim3$ kyr, the estimated recombination timescale. We revised the SNR dynamical age to $\sim8$ kyr, much younger than previous estimates. The complex structure and complex ionization state distribution may suggest that centrally filled and shell-like morphologies coexist in W28. This state may depend on the environment in which the SNR evolves.

AI.tldr: The study uses X-ray observations of W28, a thermal composite supernova remnant (SNR), revealing a shell-like structure in its western region and providing evidence for recombining plasma coinciding with Hα emissions. It suggests an isobaric and nearly isothermal environment, with a revised dynamical age of ~8 kyr due to saturated thermal conduction and cloud evaporation effects. This challenges previous assumptions about SNR morphology and environmental influences.
AI.motivation: To address why some SNRs exhibit thermal composite morphologies instead of typical shells and to explain internal recombining plasma, using W28 as a case study.
AI.method: 13-ks X-ray observation with the Following-up X-ray Telescope onboard Einstein Probe. Spectral analysis mapped plasma states, spatially correlating with Hα emissions. Analyzed temperature, pressure, and ionization states across regions, considering cooling timescales and environmental factors.
AI.result: Discovered a shell-like structure extending W28's size to 72'×45'. Plasma recombines in regions matching Hα emissions, while others remain ionization-dominated. Found isobaricity and central ~0.6-0.7 keV isothermal plasma before cooling. Estimated ~3 kyr recombination timescale via saturated conduction and cloud evaporation. Revised dynamical age to ~8 kyr, younger than prior estimates.
AI.conclusion: W28's mixed morphology (filled center + shell) arises from environmental interactions, suggesting SNR structures depend on surrounding medium. Challenges prior assumptions about SNR evolution timescales and morphology transitions.

title: On the Universal Cuspy Behavior in Black Hole Shadows
summary: This work investigates the universality of cusp formation in the shadows of compact objects. The emergence of cusps is accompanied by three interrelated phenomena: a topological charge transition, an equal-area law governing the self-intersecting structure, and universal critical scaling behavior. We demonstrate that, because these phenomena originate from the global morphology of the shadow, they are fundamentally independent of specific spacetime metric details and apply across diverse models. These features are systematically analyzed for the Kerr black hole endowed with a running Newton coupling. By extending our framework to rotating traversable wormholes, we confirm that the same universal behavior persists in more general compact objects. Our study uncovers the universality underlying cusp formation, offering a model-independent framework for identifying non-Kerr signatures in future black hole observations.

AI.tldr: The paper explores the universality of cusp formation in shadows of compact objects, identifying universal phenomena like topological charge transition, equal-area law, and critical scaling that transcend specific spacetime metrics. These are demonstrated in Kerr black holes with a running Newton coupling and extended to traversable wormholes, providing a model-independent framework for non-Kerr observation analysis.
AI.motivation: To understand if cusp formation characteristics in compact object shadows are universal, independent of specific spacetime details, enabling broader applicability in astrophysical observations.
AI.method: Analysis of Kerr black holes with a running Newton coupling and extension to rotating traversable wormholes. Examination of topological charge transitions, equal-area laws, and critical scaling in shadow morphologies.
AI.result: Confirmed universal behavior across diverse compact objects; cusp-related phenomena are metric-independent. Framework identifies non-Kerr signatures without relying on specific spacetime models.
AI.conclusion: Cusp formation universality offers a robust method for detecting deviations from Kerr models in observed black holes, enhancing astrophysical data interpretation.

title: Monte Carlo conformal prediction for quantifying uncertainty in radio galaxy classification under ambiguous ground truth
summary: Dramatically increasing data volumes are forcing astronomers to adopt automated methods for the identification and classification of astronomical objects. Although deep-learning models are often well-suited to this task, obtaining a measure of uncertainty on their predictions is challenging. Here we consider the suitability of Monte Carlo conformal prediction (MCCP) set size and confidence as measures of model uncertainty for the astronomical classification of radio galaxies. We demonstrate this approach using model predictions from a pre-trained radio galaxy foundation model, fine-tuned on a smaller set of labelled radio galaxies. We calibrate the MCCP by obtaining annotator-derived soft label distributions, i.e. probability distributions over classes instead of single class assignments, for each of these labelled radio galaxies and compare the resulting set sizes and confidence scores to predictive entropy measures for each galaxy obtained using a supervised Bayesian deep-learning model trained using Hamiltonian Monte Carlo (HMC). The comparison reveals only a weak correlation between the measures.

AI.tldr: The study evaluates Monte Carlo conformal prediction (MCCP) as a measure of uncertainty in classifying radio galaxies using a fine-tuned deep-learning model, finding only a weak correlation between MCCP metrics and predictive entropy from a Bayesian model.
AI.motivation: The increasing volume of astronomical data necessitates automated classification methods with uncertainty estimates. Existing deep-learning models often lack reliable uncertainty quantification, making MCCP and Bayesian approaches promising candidates.
AI.method: The researchers used a pre-trained radio galaxy foundation model fine-tuned on labelled data and calibrated MCCP using annotator-derived soft labels. The MCCP set sizes/confidence scores were compared against entropy metrics from an HMC-based Bayesian model.
AI.result: MCCP set sizes and confidence scores showed only a weak correlation with predictive entropy measures from the Bayesian model, indicating different uncertainty quantification behaviors.
AI.conclusion: MCCP offers a distinct measure of prediction uncertainty compared to Bayesian methods, suggesting it could complement traditional approaches in astronomical classification tasks requiring robust uncertainty estimation.

title: Understanding Bell locality tests at colliders
summary: For decades, it has been known that local hidden variable theories cannot be disproved by collider experiments involving decaying particles. However, if these theories satisfy a small set of mild assumptions, they become testable. In particular, they can be disproved using Bell-like inequalities for $μ^+ μ^-$ and $τ^+ τ^-$ pairs.

AI.tldr: The paper demonstrates that local hidden variable theories can be tested and potentially disproven using Bell-like inequalities for certain particle pairs (μ+μ− and τ+τ−) when they adhere to specific mild assumptions.
AI.motivation: To address the longstanding issue that local hidden variable theories were previously untestable via collider experiments involving decaying particles, and to establish conditions under which these theories become falsifiable.
AI.method: The authors propose applying Bell-like inequalities to analyze μ+μ− and τ+τ− particle pairs, requiring the theories to satisfy a small set of mild assumptions to enable such testing.
AI.result: The study shows that under the proposed assumptions, local hidden variable theories can indeed be experimentally disproven through the use of Bell-like inequalities, offering a new approach to challenge these theories.
AI.conclusion: The work highlights the importance of specific assumptions in rendering local hidden variable theories testable, providing a pathway for future experiments to either validate or refute these theories using collider data from appropriate particle pairs.

title: VHE gamma-ray intranight variability from BL Lacertae during the extreme flaring state of 2022
comment: Accepted for publication in A&A. 13 pages + 8 pages of Appendix
summary: BL Lacertae (BL Lac), the archetype blazar of its subclass and one of the most studied blazars in the last decades, has gone through a series of major multi-wavelength outbursts since 2020, resulting in its highest recorded $γ$-ray flare up to date between September and November 2022 together with those from August 2021 and October 2024. We characterise the $γ$-ray and multi-wavelength emission and spectral energy distribution (SED) of BL Lac, as well as their evolution during the major and extended $γ$-ray and multi-wavelength flare occurring between September and November 2022. We evaluate the variability of the flare, with focus on the nights of October 20 and November 13, when clear intranight very-high-energy (VHE, $E>100$ GeV) $γ$-ray variability is observed. We model the $γ$-ray and broadband SEDs during periods of stable emission identified with a Bayesian block analysis, interpreting their evolution of the flare from the variability of the relativistic particles and physical parameters of the jet. The VHE emission shows an average flux of 0.23 Crab Units (C.U.) above 200 GeV during this flare and a variability amplitude of more than a factor 10. Intranight doubling-flux variations as fast as $\sim$8 minutes are observed during the nights of October 20 and November 13, when maximum fluxes of 4.4 C.U. above 100 GeV and 2.8 C.U. above 200 GeV are reached. The spectral analysis reveals a transition of the X-ray emission from the high- to the low-energy SED peak, and a shift of the $γ$-ray peak towards higher energies. The broadband emission was interpreted within a leptonic two-zone model in which intranight variability is explained as magnetic reconnection in a compact region closely oriented with the line of sight while weekly-scale variations can be explained as variations of the electron distributions and the injection of accelerated particles.

AI.tldr: The paper analyzes major multi-wavelength outbursts of BL Lacertae between 2020-2024, focusing on the 2022 flare. It observes extreme VHE γ-ray variability (flux variations up to 10x, 8-minute doubling times), models SED evolution with a two-zone leptonic model linking intranight flares to magnetic reconnection and long-term changes to particle injection.
AI.motivation: To understand the extreme variability mechanisms and broadband emission properties of BL Lacertae during unprecedented flares, providing insights into relativistic jet dynamics in blazars.
AI.method: Characterized γ-ray/multi-wavelength data, performed Bayesian block analysis to identify stable emission periods, modeled SEDs using a leptonic two-zone model evaluating particle and jet parameter variations.
AI.result: Observed intranight VHE γ-ray flux doubling in 8 minutes (4.4 C.U. peak at 100 GeV), SED showed X-ray peak shift, γ-ray peak shift to higher energies; variability interpreted via magnetic reconnection for short-term and particle injection for weekly changes.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates extreme energy production in blazar jets, validates two-zone model for explaining multi-timescale variability, highlights importance of magnetic reconnection and particle acceleration in transient high-energy phenomena.

title: Microstate Counting for rotating (type~II) isolated horizons
summary: We present a proposal for black hole microstate counting in Loop Quantum Gravity (LQG) for rotating (type~II) isolated horizons. The key obstacle in extending the standard nonrotating entropy derivation arises from the $θ$-dependent rotation 1-form, which breaks the global Chern--Simons (CS) structure on the horizon. We propose a local decomposition of the horizon $S^2$ into narrow concentric rings, each approximated as a locally nonrotating patch with a constant effective CS level. Each ring is quantized independently using standard LQG techniques, and the total entropy is obtained by integrating over the entire horizon. This method restores a local CS description, includes the contribution of angular momentum, and is consistent with the first law of black hole mechanics.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a method to count microstates of rotating black holes in Loop Quantum Gravity by decomposing the horizon into nonrotating rings, each with a constant effective Chern-Simons level, thereby recovering entropy consistent with the first law.
AI.motivation: To overcome the challenge of extending nonrotating black hole entropy calculations to rotating cases in LQG, where the rotation breaks the global Chern-Simons structure necessary for the standard derivation.
AI.method: The horizon S² is divided into concentric rings, each treated as a locally nonrotating patch with a constant effective Chern-Simons level. Each ring is quantized separately using LQG techniques, and total entropy is integrated over the entire horizon.
AI.result: The approach successfully restores a local Chern-Simons description, incorporates angular momentum effects, and yields entropy consistent with the first law of black hole mechanics.
AI.conclusion: This decomposition method provides a viable framework for calculating rotating black hole microstate entropy in LQG, addressing key theoretical barriers posed by rotational dynamics.

title: Theory of optical long-baseline interferometry on polarized sources
comment: 6 pages, no figure, accepted for publication by A&A
summary: The effects of the polarization characteristics of beam trains in optical long-baseline interferometers are well known and have led to difficulties in measuring the spatial coherence of astronomical sources in the past. This has been overcome by designing symmetrical optical trains. With the advent of interferometers using large telescopes, observations of faint sources with high degrees of polarization have become even more possible. As in the radio domain, where radiation processes usually lead to high polarization rates, a description of coherence for polarized or unpolarized sources observed with non-polarization neutral interferometers is necessary. A theory of optical long-baseline interferometry fully taking into account the polarization characteristics of beam trains and those of the sources is presented in this paper, building on concepts developed for radio aperture synthesis. The concept of generalized Mueller matrix is introduced for the case of multi-aperture interferometers leading to a simple matrix relationship between the observed Stokes visibilities, as they are disturbed by the instrument polarization characteristics, and the object Stokes visibilities. This relationship is applied to the case of single-mode interferometers. The formalism also shows that classical complex visibilities (squared moduli, phases and closure phases) need to be debiased from polarization crosstalk, even when the source is not polarized as in this case ghost polarized visibilities are created.

AI.tldr: This paper presents a comprehensive theory for optical long-baseline interferometry that fully accounts for polarization characteristics of beam trains and sources, introducing a generalized Mueller matrix approach. It addresses polarization crosstalk effects in interferometric measurements, showing that classical visibility data must be debiased even for non-polarized sources to avoid artifacts.
AI.motivation: To overcome historical measurement challenges in spatial coherence due to beam train polarization effects and accommodate observations of faint, highly polarized astronomical sources with modern large interferometers, similar to advancements in radio interferometry.
AI.method: Develops a generalized Mueller matrix formalism for multi-aperture interferometers, establishing a matrix relationship between observed and object Stokes visibilities. Applies this framework to single-mode interferometers, extending radio aperture synthesis concepts to optical systems.
AI.result: Demonstrated that polarization characteristics inherently affect observed Stokes visibilities, even for unpolarized sources. Reveals ghost polarized visibilities created by instrumental polarization crosstalk necessitating debiasing of classical complex visibilities (moduli, phases, closure phases) before analysis.
AI.conclusion: Polarization effects must be fully integrated into optical interferometry data analysis to accurately measure spatial coherence and avoid spurious results. The proposed Mueller matrix framework provides the necessary tools for unbiased interpretation of interferometric observations.

title: Baryogenesis and Dark Matter from light Sterile Neutrinos
summary: We propose a simple and flexible mechanism by which sterile neutrinos with masses below the electroweak scale can simultaneously account for the observed baryon asymmetry of the Universe and the dark matter abundance. Crucially, neutrinos in this mass range behave as Dirac particles at high temperatures, allowing connections to Dirac leptogenesis, while at low temperatures, they can serve as viable warm dark matter candidates. We first perform a general analysis, assuming that unspecified ultraviolet dynamics generate both symmetric and asymmetric sterile-neutrino abundances before decoupling. Treating these abundances as initial conditions for the subsequent evolution allows us to systematically explore the phenomenologically viable regions of the low-energy parameter space, taking into account cosmological and astrophysical constraints, as well as implications for light-neutrino mass generation. Finally, we illustrate the model-building opportunities enabled by this minimal setup by studying two specific ultraviolet completions.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a mechanism where sterile neutrinos below the electroweak scale explain both the universe's baryon asymmetry and dark matter. These neutrinos act as Dirac particles at high temps (linking to leptogenesis) and warm dark matter at low temps. The authors analyze viable parameter regions under cosmological constraints and present two model examples.
AI.motivation: To reconcile two major cosmological puzzles—baryon asymmetry and dark matter abundance—using a single particle framework (sterile neutrinos). Existing mechanisms often address these separately, so this work seeks a unified solution within a minimal particle physics model.
AI.method: 1. Assume ultraviolet dynamics generate both symmetric and asymmetric sterile neutrino abundances pre-decoupling. 2. Use these as initial conditions for cosmological evolution. 3. Apply cosmological/astrophysical constraints (e.g., CMB, structure formation) and neutrino mass implications. 4. Test with two specific UV completions to demonstrate feasibility.
AI.result: Identified parameter regions where sterile neutrinos successfully account for both baryon asymmetry and warm dark matter. Showed model independence by decoupling UV details initially, then demonstrating valid completions.
AI.conclusion: Sterile neutrinos in this mass range offer a viable, unified explanation for two cosmological problems. The approach provides a framework for future model-building with clear observational tests, emphasizing the importance of low-energy parameters and UV completeness.

title: High-energy neutrino flux from SN2024ggi: constraints from semi-analytic modeling of its post-explosive emission
comment: 4 pages, 1 figure, accepted for publication in "Il Nuovo Cimento - Colloquia and Communications in Physics"
summary: Hydrogen-rich supernovae can efficiently accelerate particles when the expanding ejecta interact with the surrounding circumstellar medium (CSM), producing high-energy (TeV--PeV) neutrinos. In this work we investigate the nearby SN~2024ggi, whose proximity and clear signatures of ejecta--CSM interaction make it a promising candidate for studying high-energy ($ν$) emission. We apply a new semi-analytical model that consistently links the electromagnetic and neutrino emission components, allowing us to constrain the main explosion parameters, including the kinetic energy, ejecta mass, progenitor radius, and nickel yield. The predicted high-energy ($ν$) fluence at Earth peaks at TeV energies and remains below the sensitivity of current detectors. However, the modeling establishes a robust framework for interpreting future signals from nearby interacting supernovae and fine-tuning observational strategies for next-generation multi-messenger facilities such as IceCube-Gen2 and KM3NeT/ARCA.

AI.tldr: Hydrogen-rich supernova SN 2024ggi is analyzed for its potential high-energy neutrino emission using a new model, showing detectability limits with current instruments but promising future applications.
AI.motivation: To study high-energy neutrino emission from nearby interacting supernovae and refine observational strategies for next-generation detectors.
AI.method: A semi-analytical model linking electromagnetic and neutrino emissions is applied to constrain explosion parameters like kinetic energy, ejecta mass, progenitor radius, and nickel yield.
AI.result: Predicted neutrino fluence peaks at TeV but remains undetectable by current detectors; establishes a framework for future analyses.
AI.conclusion: SN 2024ggi demonstrates the model's utility for interpreting future supernova signals and optimizing next-gen multi-messenger observations.

title: Chaotic motion and power spectral density in Schwarzschild Bertotti-Robinson black hole spacetime
summary: In this paper, we show that in weak field limit Schwarzschild Bertotti-Robinson black hole (Schwarzschild-BR BH) turns into Schwarzschild black hole immersed in external uniform magnetic field which is given in 1. The dynamics of both magnetized and electrically charged particles in the vicinity of a Schwarzschild-BR black hole are investigated. The innermost stable circular orbits (ISCOs) for both magnetized and electrically charged particles are examined in detail, revealing that the magnetic field parameter B exerts a considerable influence, leading to an increase in the ISCO radius. The orbital and epicyclic motion of test particles in Schwarzschild-BR black hole spacetime was analyzed, including both circular orbits and their oscillatory perturbations. Additionally, the trajectories of both magnetized and electrically charged particles are analyzed for various configurations of the magnetic parameter B. We also demonstrate how the magnetic field B, electric charge q, and magnetic moment μ influence the dynamics of charged particles, specifically affecting the chaotic behavior, Poincare' sections, oscillatory frequencies and power spectral density.

AI.tldr: This paper examines the dynamics of magnetized and charged particles around a Schwarzschild-BR black hole, showing that external magnetic fields significantly affect orbital parameters like ISCO radii and influence chaotic behavior through parameters B, q, and μ.
AI.motivation: To understand how external magnetic fields modify particle dynamics near black holes, extending Schwarzschild solutions to include magnetic effects and studying their impacts on orbital stability and chaos.
AI.method: Analyzed orbital and epicyclic motion of particles in Schwarzschild-BR spacetime under varying magnetic fields, used ISCO calculations, Poincaré sections, and spectral analysis to assess chaos and frequency changes.
AI.result: Magnetic field parameter B increases ISCO radius, alters particle trajectories, and induces chaotic behavior via interactions with particle charges and moments.
AI.conclusion: External magnetic fields play a crucial role in shaping both orbital structures and dynamical chaos around black holes, highlighting the necessity of considering electromagnetic influences in astrophysical models.

title: Global Analyses of Generalized Parton Distributions with Diverse PDF Inputs
summary: This paper investigates the crucial role of parton distribution functions (PDFs) in high-energy physics, particularly their impact on the extraction of generalized parton distributions (GPDs) at zero skewness. To this aim, we perform six global analyses of GPDs using different modern PDF sets (\texttt{NNPDF40}, \texttt{CT18}, and \texttt{MSHT20}) at three specific factorization scales ($ μ= 2 $, $ 1.3 $, and $ 1 $ GeV) and different perturbative orders. A wide range of elastic electron scattering data is included in the analysis to constrain GPDs in a broad interval in the momentum transfer squared $ t $. The analyses reveal that the best overall description of experimental data is achieved using \texttt{NNPDF40} PDFs at the next-to-leading order (NLO), with moderate sensitivity to the choice of PDF input, especially in the region of low $ t $. The dependence on the perturbative order is relatively mild, indicating stability in the extraction procedure. We also show that different GPD sets become more consistent at larger $ |t| $ values, with down-quark GPDs experiencing greater suppression than up-quark GPDs. The six extracted GPD sets, available at different scales and perturbative orders, provide valuable resources for future theoretical and phenomenological studies, offering flexibility for researchers in exploring the proton's internal structure.

AI.tldr: This paper explores the impact of modern PDF sets and perturbative orders on GPD extraction at zero skewness, finding NNPDF40 at NLO provides the best description of data, with PDF choice sensitivity strongest at low t and GPD consistency improving at higher |t|.
AI.motivation: To understand how parton distribution functions (PDFs) influence the extraction of generalized parton distributions (GPDs), particularly at zero skewness, and assess the stability and sensitivity of GPD determinations under varying PDF sets and theoretical parameters.
AI.method: Six global GPD analyses using PDF sets (NNPDF40, CT18, MSHT20) across three factorization scales (μ = 2, 1.3, 1 GeV) and different perturbative orders. Elastic electron scattering data was included to constrain GPDs over a broad t range.
AI.result: NNPDF40 at NLO best describes data. PDF choice sensitivity is strongest at low t. Perturbative order dependence is mild. GPD sets converge at larger |t|, with down-quark GPDs more suppressed than up-quark GPDs.
AI.conclusion: Six GPD sets provide flexible tools for probing proton structure. Results highlight PDF and scale dependencies, guiding future studies toward optimal parameter choices and interpretations of GPD behavior.

title: Early emission characterization of TDE2025aarm
summary:
In this Letter, we present early emission data analysis of the tidal disruption event TDE2025aarm, including optical, UV and X-ray data.
  At a redshift of z = 0.01368, TDE2025aarm is the second closest TDE ever discovered, offering an unprecedented opportunity to study such phenomena in great details.
  We observed TDE2025aarm in optical with the Liverpool Telescope for a total of three epochs, and complemented our dataset with ancillary spectroscopic and photometric data.
  The early optical spectra are characterized by a blue-continuum and helium, hydrogen and possibly Bowen lines typical of H+He events.
  The optical light curves peak at M_g ~ -18.63 mag and are well described by fallback of a M_star ~ 0.16 M_sun star onto a M_BH ~ 2x10^{7} M_sun black hole.
  We report Swift-XRT detection in the 0.3-10 keV range, with a total flux of F_X ~ 1.42x10^{-14} erg s-1 cm-2, fitted by a black-body with kT ~ 0.39 keV.
  This makes TDE2025aarm a new event among optical/UV bright TDEs detected in soft X-rays.
  Our analysis suggests that the early emission from TDE2025aarm is powered by circularization shocks, and that the delayed accretion scenario best describes the observed features.

AI.tldr: The analysis of TDE2025aarm, an optically bright tidal disruption event (TDE) at z = 0.01368, combines optical, UV, and Swift-XRT X-ray data. The early emission is best explained by circularization shocks and a delayed accretion model, with parameters indicating a 0.16 solar-mass star disrupted by a 2x10^7 solar-mass black hole.
AI.motivation: Study the closest TDEs to understand tidal disruption mechanics, accretion processes, and emission mechanisms in unprecedented detail due to its proximity.
AI.method: Multiwavelength observations using the Liverpool Telescope (optical) and Swift-XRT (X-ray). Spectroscopic analysis of optical spectra showing H/He lines andBowen fluorescence, combined with light curve modeling of optical emission peaks.
AI.result: Confirmed detection of soft X-rays via blackbody fit (kT ~0.39 keV), optical light curves peaking at Mg ~ -18.63 mag matching fallback model parameters. Observations align with delayed accretion and circularization shock predictions.
AI.conclusion: TDE2025aarm provides strong evidence for circularization shock dominance in early TDE emission and supports delayed accretion models, advancing understanding of TDE energetics and observational signatures.

title: Casimir-Induced Quintessence in Dark Dimension
summary: We investigate a concrete realization of the Dark Dimension scenario, where a single large extra dimension is set at sub-millimeter scales. In this framework, the Casimir energy of bulk fields accounts for the observed dark energy. Working in a 5-dimensional setup with the Standard Model confined to a 4-dimensional brane, we derive the effective action for the radion. We demonstrate that a minimal model comprising only gravity and three right-handed bulk neutrinos typically yields a negative radion potential. To realize a positive vacuum energy, we consider some extensions with additional bulk degrees of freedom. These extensions generate a sufficiently flat positive potential that allows the radion to behave as a quintessence field, evolving slowly at the sub-eV scale. Finally, we analyze the evolution of the dark-energy equation-of-state parameter and show that our model is consistent with recent DESI BAO measurements, including the distance ratios $D_H/r_d$ and $D_M/r_d$.

AI.tldr: The paper explores a Dark Dimension scenario with a large extra dimension at sub-millimeter scales, where bulk Casimir energy explains dark energy. The model uses a 5D setup with Standard Model fields on a 4D brane and finds that adding bulk neutrinos leads to a negative radion potential, requiring extensions to generate a positive potential compatible with dark energy observations.
AI.motivation: To address the cosmological constant problem and reconcile dark energy observations with models of large extra dimensions.
AI.method: Derive the effective radion action in a 5D brane-world model, analyze the effect of bulk fields (neutrinos and extensions) on the radion potential, and simulate cosmological evolution of the equation-of-state parameter.
AI.result: A minimal model gives negative radion potential; extensions with extra bulk degrees of freedom create a positive, flat potential allowing radion to act as sub-eV quintessence. The model's predictions match DESI BAO distance ratio measurements.
AI.conclusion: Dark Dimension scenarios with properly extended bulk fields can naturally explain dark energy through radion dynamics, consistent with observational constraints on late-time cosmic expansion.

title: Right-Handed Leptonic Mixing and Enhancement Bands in Left-Right Symmetry
summary: Left-right (LR) symmetric theories predict right-handed charged currents whose flavor structure encodes the realization of parity. While the right-handed quark mixing matrix closely tracks its left-handed counterpart, the leptonic sector with purely Dirac neutrinos has remained structurally unclear. We show that, in contrast to the quark case, parity in the Dirac leptonic sector generically induces branch-dependent enhancement bands in which RH-LH misalignment becomes parametrically large despite small parity breaking. We derive analytic solutions of the LR consistency equation and demonstrate that the interplay between spontaneous parity violation and spectral near-degeneracies leads to a qualitatively new pattern of right-handed mixing. This establishes the Dirac leptonic sector of the minimal LR theory as a predictive and structurally distinct regime.

AI.tldr: This paper explores the leptonic sector in left-right symmetric theories with Dirac neutrinos, revealing unexpected patterns in right-handed charged currents and their mixing matrices.
AI.motivation: To understand the flavor structure of right-handed charged currents in the Dirac leptonic sector and clarify how parity realization affects their mixing compared to the quark sector.
AI.method: Derive analytic solutions of the LR consistency equation, analyze the interplay between spontaneous parity violation and spectral near-degeneracies, and examine branch-dependent enhancement bands.
AI.result: Parity in the Dirac leptonic sector causes parametrically large RH-LH misalignment in specific branches due to near-degeneracies, leading to a new mixing pattern distinct from quarks.
AI.conclusion: The minimal LR theory's Dirac leptonic sector is structurally unique and predictive, offering a clear framework for testing parity models via right-handed charged current phenomena.

title: Numerically stable equations for the orbital evolution of compact object binaries
summary: The orbital and eccentricity evolution for compact object binaries through gravitational wave emission first derived by Peters and Mathews are used extensively throughout the gravitational wave community for calculating the orbital evolution and merger time of compact binaries. While improved calculations of the binary merger time have been the focus of several investigations since, the orbital evolution has not received the same attention. As the equations lack a closed form solution, a numerical integrator is required, but standard methods typically break when the point of merger is overstepped. We present a rewrite of Peters' equations in $\ln$-space, which allows common numerical solvers to converge. This leads to a more numerically robust and computationally efficient method for evolving compact binaries due to gravitational wave emission, reducing the number of function evaluations by 60\% to 70\% in our tests.

AI.tldr: The paper presents a reformulation of Peters' equations in logarithmic space to improve the numerical integration of compact binary orbital evolution, enhancing computational efficiency and stability.
AI.motivation: Standard methods struggle with numerical stability when integrating Peters' equations past the merger point, leading to inefficiencies.
AI.method: Rewriting the equations in logarithmic (ln)-space to enable robust convergence with common numerical solvers.
AI.result: The new approach reduces function evaluations by 60-70% in tests while maintaining accuracy.
AI.conclusion: This method provides a more efficient and stable tool for calculating gravitational wave-driven binary evolution without overstepping merger points.

title: Modeling the merger-ringdown of an eccentric test-mass inspiral into a Kerr black hole using the effective-one-body framework
summary: We characterize and phenomenologically model the merger-ringdown of gravitational waves emitted by a small compact object that plunges and merges into a Kerr black hole from equatorial-eccentric inspirals. The waveforms are generated employing a time-domain Teukolsky code sourced with trajectories computed using the effective-one-body framework. We span values of the Kerr spin $a\in[-0.9, 0.9] $, eccentricity at the last stable orbit (LSO) $ e_{\rm LSO} \in [0,0.9] $, and relativistic anomaly $ ξ_{\rm LSO} \in [0 , 2 π]$. We characterize the last peak of the waveform and ringdown features across the parameter space, finding that the eccentricity mainly affects the last peak features, while it has a smaller impact on the ringdown signal. In contrast, the relativistic anomaly measured at the LSO influences the morphology of the last peak in a restricted portion of the parameter space and has no impact on the ringdown part. We perform the analysis for all the spin-weighted spherical harmonic modes normally included in the \texttt{SEOBNR} family of models, $(\ell,m)\in\{ (2,2), (3,3), (4,4), (5,5), (2,1), (3,2), (4,3)\}$. Finally, we introduce a merger-ringdown model for \texttt{SEOB-TMLE}, a forthcoming inspiral-merger-ringdown waveform model for eccentric spin-aligned binary black holes in the test-mass limit, whose features can be extended to comparable-mass regimes. The model also accounts for quasinormal mode mixing during the ringdown. It provides a first step toward incorporating the impact of residual eccentricity close to merger into spin-aligned effective-one-body merger-ringdown models for binary black holes.

AI.tldr: The paper studies gravitational wave signals from eccentric inspirals of a small object into a Kerr black hole using numerical and analytical methods, focusing on merger-ringdown phases. It explores how parameters like spin, eccentricity, and anomaly affect waveform features, providing insights for improving waveform models like SEOB-TMLE.
AI.motivation: To better understand and model the effects of eccentricity and Kerr spin on gravitational wave signals during merger and ringdown, essential for detecting and analyzing black hole mergers with eccentric orbits, particularly in the test-mass limit.
AI.method: Using a time-domain Teukolsky code with trajectories computed via the effective-one-body (EOB) framework to generate waveforms. Parameters varied include Kerr spin (a), eccentricity at LSO (e_LSO), and relativistic anomaly (ξ_LSO). Analyzed multiple spherical harmonic modes and developed a merger-ringdown model for SEOB-TMLE.
